verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to Jonah the son of Amittai, saying,
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "the word of YHWH came" (wayehi devar-YHWH) is a standard prophetic formula appearing throughout the Twelve Prophets and the Former Prophets, marking divine commissioning. The name "Jonah" (yonah) means "dove," a detail with possible symbolic resonance given his role as a reluctant messenger. "Amittai" (amittay) means "my truth" or "my faithfulness," a name of ironic weight given Jonah's flight from YHWH's commission. (2 Kgs 14:25)

words.g: the + in/with a city in the widest sense

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: "Arise, go to Nineveh, that great city, and call out against her, for their evil has come up before my face."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Nineveh was the capital of the Neo-Assyrian Empire, one of the most formidable and feared powers of the ancient Near East, located on the eastern bank of the Tigris River in modern-day northern Iraq. The phrase "their evil has come up before my face" echoes the language of Gen 18:20-21, where the outcry of Sodom rises to YHWH — positioning Nineveh typologically alongside the archetypal wicked city. THEOLOGICAL: The universality of YHWH's moral governance is asserted here: the God of Israel holds pagan nations accountable for their wickedness, a theme that runs through the prophets (Amos 1-2, Nahum).

verses.flow: But Jonah arose to flee to Tarshish from the face of YHWH, and he went down to Joppa and found a ship going to Tarshish. And he paid her fare and went down into it, to go with them to Tarshish, away from the face of YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The repetition of "from the face of YHWH" (mippene YHWH) twice in this verse is theologically pointed — Jonah's flight is not merely geographic but is a rejection of covenantal presence and prophetic calling. The verb "went down" (yarad) is used three times in vv. 3-5, creating a descent motif that culminates in the depths of the sea — a literary and theological unraveling. TYPOLOGY: As Christ descended into death and the heart of the earth, Jonah's descent prefigures that pattern in reverse — Jonah flees into the deep, while Christ willingly enters it.

verses.flow: But YHWH hurled a great wind upon the sea, and there was a great storm (se'arah) on the sea, and the ship was about to be broken to pieces.

verses.flow: Then the mariners were afraid, and each man cried out to his god, and they hurled the cargo that was in the ship into the sea to lighten it for them. But Jonah had gone down into the innermost parts of the vessel and had lain down and fallen into a deep sleep (tardemah).
verses.note: HEBREW: The word "tardemah" (Strong's H8639) denotes a supernaturally deep sleep, the same word used in Gen 2:21 when YHWH causes Adam to sleep before drawing out Eve, and in Gen 15:12 during the covenant with Abraham. Its use here may suggest Jonah's detachment from reality and covenant responsibility. TYPOLOGY: Jonah asleep in the storm-tossed vessel directly parallels Jesus asleep in the boat during the storm on the Sea of Galilee (Mark 4:38), a connection the gospel writers likely intended as deliberate typological echo.

verses.flow: So the captain of the ship came to him and said to him, "What do you mean, O sleeper? Arise, call out to your God! Perhaps the God will give thought to us, that we may not perish."

verses.flow: And each man said to his companion, "Come, let us cast lots, that we may know on whose account this evil has come upon us." So they cast lots, and the lot fell upon Jonah.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The casting of lots (goralot) was a widely practiced method of divination throughout the ancient Near East to determine divine will or assign blame. Within Israelite theology, lots were understood to be under YHWH's sovereign direction (Prov 16:33), so the outcome is not mere chance but providential disclosure. The lot's fall upon Jonah functions as a divine verdict rendered through pagan means — YHWH's sovereignty extending even over the sailors' casting.

verses.flow: Then they said to him, "Tell us now, on whose account has this evil come upon us? What is your occupation, and from where do you come? What is your land, and from what people are you?"

verses.flow: And he said to them, "I am a Hebrew (Ivri), and YHWH, the God of the heavens, I fear — who made the sea and the dry land."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ivri" (Hebrew, Strong's H5680) is the ethnic self-designation Jonah uses, rooted possibly in the name "Eber" (Gen 10:24) or in the term for one who "crosses over." This is a rare instance of an Israelite identifying himself by this term to foreigners. His confession — "YHWH, the God of the heavens, I fear, who made the sea and the dry land" — is richly ironic: Jonah confesses YHWH's sovereignty over the very sea he is fleeing across. THEOLOGICAL: The confession signals that Jonah's flight is not one of ignorance but willful rebellion against a God he knows to be omnipotent.

verses.flow: Then the men were exceedingly afraid, and they said to him, "What is this that you have done?" For the men knew that he was fleeing from the face of YHWH, because he had told them.

verses.flow: Therefore they called out to YHWH and said, "O YHWH, let us not perish, we pray, on account of this man's life, and lay not upon us innocent blood — for you, O YHWH, have done as it pleased you."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The sailors' prayer to YHWH by name marks a remarkable theological moment — pagan mariners, who had each cried to their own god in v. 5, now address Israel's God directly by his covenant name. Their concern for "innocent blood" reflects awareness of moral law even outside the covenant community. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The language of accountability before YHWH as cosmic sovereign echoes the framework of Deut 32:8-9, wherein the nations are under both divine and mediating governance — yet YHWH remains the ultimate judge before whom all blood-guilt is reckoned.

verses.flow: Then the men feared with a great fear (yir'ah gedolah) YHWH, and they offered a sacrifice to YHWH and made vows.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Yir'ah gedolah" — a great fear — is the same construction used in Exod 14:31 after Israel witnesses YHWH's power at the sea. The sailors' response of sacrifice and vows constitutes genuine religious conversion in the narrative's presentation. THEOLOGICAL: These pagan sailors come to fear and worship YHWH through the witness of a disobedient prophet — a profound irony that previews the book's theme that YHWH's mercy and revelation extend beyond Israel's borders (cf. Matt 8:11).

verses.flow: And Jonah prayed unto YHWH his God from the belly of the fish.

words.m: conjunction + verb hithpael sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And he said: "I called out from my distress unto YHWH, and He answered me; from the belly of Sheol (she'ol) I cried for help — You heard my voice.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "From the belly of Sheol I cried" — Jonah's prayer from the depths of the fish's belly is the primary typological anchor for Jesus' declaration in Matt 12:40: "as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so will the Son of Man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth." The Hebrew she'ol (H7585) refers to the realm of the dead, giving the descent a genuine mortuary weight beyond mere distress. THEOLOGICAL: Jonah's deliverance through death-and-return structures the entire theology of resurrection as YHWH's sovereign answer to the cry of the righteous from the lowest place.

words.g: Hades or the world of the dead

verses.flow: And You cast me into the deep, into the heart of the seas, and the flood (nahar) surrounded me; all Your breakers and Your waves passed over me.
verses.note: HEBREW: The Hebrew nahar (H5104, "river" or "flood") here carries cosmic-ocean imagery drawn from ancient Near Eastern cosmology, where the chaotic deep-waters represent the realm opposing divine order. The phrase "all Your breakers and Your waves" (kol-mishbarekha we-gallekha) closely echoes Ps 42:7, suggesting a liturgical tradition of describing divine testing through overwhelming waters. DIVINE_COUNCIL: In the cosmological background of Ugaritic and Second Temple texts, the sea (yam) and its floods represent the chaotic anti-creation domain, here explicitly reframed as under YHWH's sovereign command — it is YHWH's breakers that overwhelm, not autonomous chaos.

words.m: verb poel imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: And I said, I am driven away from before Your eyes; yet again I will look upon Your holy temple.

words.g: to drive out from a possession

verses.flow: Waters encompassed me to the point of death (nephesh); the deep (tehom) closed around me; weeds were wrapped about my head.
verses.note: HEBREW: Nephesh (H5315) here is translated "death" following the idiom le-nephesh — literally "to the soul/throat," an idiom for life-threatening extremity. Tehom (H8415, "the deep") is the same primordial deep of Gen 1:2, invoking the pre-creation abyss. THEOLOGICAL: Jonah's descent into the watery tehom recapitulates creation-in-reverse: the prophet sinks through the cosmic deep, mirroring the chaos from which YHWH ordered the world, making his retrieval a new-creation act.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix
verb poel imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: To the roots of the mountains I descended; the earth — her bars were upon me forever; yet You brought up my life from the pit (shachat), O YHWH my God.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "You brought up my life from the pit (shachat)" — shachat (H7845) denotes the pit of destruction, synonymous with death and corruption. This language is directly echoed in Ps 16:10, "You will not abandon my soul to Sheol, nor let your Holy One see corruption," a verse Peter explicitly applies to the resurrection of Jesus in Acts 2:27. Jonah's ascent from the pit thus foreshadows the resurrection of the Messiah who descends fully into death and is raised incorruptible.

words.g: a mountain or range of hills
in up to or over against

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: When my soul (nephesh) was faint within me, I remembered YHWH, and my prayer came unto You, unto Your holy temple.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: Those who observe (shamar) vanities of emptiness (hebel) forsake their own steadfast love (hesed).
verses.note: HEBREW: Hebel (H1892) means "vapor, breath, vanity" — the same term that frames Ecclesiastes ("vanity of vanities"). The phrase "vanities of emptiness" (shawe' hebel) is a double intensification meaning utterly empty falsehoods, here referring to idols. Hesed (H2617) — steadfast covenant-love, loyalty, lovingkindness — is the great relational term of the covenant. THEOLOGICAL: The verse declares that idolatry is not merely moral failure but covenantal self-abandonment: to cling to empty things is to forfeit one's own hesed — the very relational identity one holds within YHWH's covenant community.

verses.flow: But as for me — with a voice of thanksgiving I will sacrifice unto You; that which I have vowed I will fulfill. Salvation (yeshu'ah) belongs to YHWH!"
verses.note: HEBREW: Yeshu'ah (H3444, "salvation, deliverance") shares its root with the name Yeshua (Jesus), making this closing doxology linguistically resonant with the NT proclamation. The declaration "salvation belongs to YHWH" (le-YHWH ha-yeshu'ah) is a covenantal affirmation that deliverance is not earned or seized but received as YHWH's sovereign gift. TYPOLOGY: Jonah's vow to sacrifice with thanksgiving from inside the fish — before his physical deliverance — models faith that anticipates resurrection, paralleling Christ's own trust in the Father through death (Ps 22:24; Heb 5:7).

verses.flow: And YHWH spoke unto the fish, and it vomited out Jonah upon the dry land.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's direct speech to the fish (va-yomer YHWH la-dag) underscores the absolute sovereignty of YHWH over creation — the great sea creature, instrument of judgment, becomes instrument of restoration at a word. HISTORICAL: Ancient Near Eastern texts (Ugaritic, Mesopotamian) portray the sea and its creatures as autonomous or semi-divine powers; the text deliberately presents the fish as wholly obedient to YHWH's command, demythologizing the chaos-sea motif. STRUCTURE: The vomiting of Jonah onto dry land (yabbashah) inverts the narrative of chapter 1 — the descent into the sea is reversed into emergence onto land, structurally enacting death-and-resurrection before Jonah arrives at Nineveh.

conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to Jonah a second time, saying,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "a second time" (Heb. שֵׁנִית, shenit) is theologically loaded — YHWH's recommission of Jonah demonstrates the irresistibility of divine calling and the grace of second chances. This parallels Peter's restoration (John 21:15-19) and underscores that YHWH's redemptive purposes cannot be thwarted by human flight or failure.

verses.flow: And Jonah arose and went to Nineveh, according to the word of YHWH. Now Nineveh was a great city to God (elohim), a journey of three days.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "A great city to elohim" (עִיר־גְּדוֹלָה לֵאלֹהִים) likely employs a Semitic superlative idiom, meaning "an exceedingly great city," though some scholars read it as indicating divine ownership or cosmic significance. Archaeological evidence from ancient Nineveh (modern Kuyunjik, near Mosul) confirms a massive metropolis; the "three days' journey" likely refers to its administrative district, not the city proper. Nineveh served as the Assyrian capital under Sennacherib (ca. 700 BC).

conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And Jonah began to enter into the city a journey of one day, and he called out and said, "Yet forty days and Nineveh shall be overthrown!"
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb הָפַךְ (hafak, Strong's H2015), translated "overthrown," is the same verb used for the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah (Gen 19:25). Forty (אַרְבָּעִים, arba'im) is a number of trial and probation throughout Scripture — forty years in the wilderness, forty days of Moses on Sinai, forty days of Elijah's journey. The brevity and bluntness of Jonah's proclamation is striking: only five Hebrew words in the original.

verses.flow: And the men of Nineveh believed in God (elohim), and they called a fast and put on sackcloth, from the greatest of them even to the least of them.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The repentance of Nineveh at the preaching of Jonah stands as a type prefiguring the Gentile reception of the gospel. Jesus explicitly invokes this event: "The men of Nineveh will rise up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, for they repented at the preaching of Jonah, and behold, something greater than Jonah is here" (Matt 12:41). The faith of pagan Nineveh condemns Israel's hardness of heart before the greater Prophet.

verses.flow: But let man and beast be covered with sackcloth, and let them call out mightily to God (elohim). And let each one turn from his evil way and from the violence (hamas) that is in his hands.
verses.note: HEBREW: הָמָס (hamas, Strong's H2555) denotes violent wrongdoing, injustice, and lawless oppression — a morally loaded term also used of the pre-flood generation's corruption (Gen 6:11). The call to turn (שׁוּב, shuv, Strong's H7725) from hamas represents genuine covenantal repentance language, though addressed to a Gentile nation, underscoring YHWH's universal moral governance over all peoples.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Who knows? God (elohim) may turn and relent and turn from his burning anger, and we shall not perish."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Who knows?" (מִי־יוֹדֵעַ, mi-yodea) reflects genuine theological humility — the king does not presume upon divine mercy but appeals to it. This mirrors Joel 2:14, using nearly identical language in a Jewish context. The relenting of God (נָחַם, nacham, Strong's H5162) is not a change in his eternal nature but a covenantal responsiveness: when the conditions that warranted judgment change, so does the outworking of his purposes in history.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

words.g: the gods in the ordinary sense

verses.flow: And God (elohim) saw their deeds, that they had turned from their evil way. And God (elohim) relented of the calamity that he had spoken of bringing upon them, and he did not do it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The verb וַיִּנָּחֶם (vayyinachem), from נָחַם (nacham), is anthropopathic language — God responding in a manner analogous to human relenting. This is theologically consistent with the Mosaic revelation of YHWH's character in Exodus 34:6-7 as compassionate and slow to anger. Jonah's anger at this mercy in chapter 4 reveals that he understood perfectly what YHWH was doing — and resented it, exposing the ethnocentric failure of his own heart.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: But it was evil to Jonah — a great evil — and it burned hot to him.
verses.note: HEBREW: "It was evil to Jonah" uses the root ra'ah (H7489/H7451), creating a deliberate wordplay: YHWH "relents of the evil (ra'ah)" he threatened (3:10), yet Jonah finds this very mercy to be "evil" to himself. The narrator exposes the grotesque irony of the prophet's heart through shared vocabulary.

words.m: conjunction + verb hithpael sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And he prayed to YHWH and said, "Ah, YHWH! Was not this my word when I was yet upon my own soil? Therefore I hastened to flee to Tarshish, for I knew that you are a God gracious (channun) and compassionate (rachum), slow to anger and great in lovingkindness (hesed), and one who relents concerning evil.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jonah's prayer quotes the foundational divine self-disclosure of Exodus 34:6-7 — the "thirteen attributes of mercy" in Jewish tradition. Jonah knew YHWH's character perfectly; his flight was not ignorance but active resistance to God's grace extending beyond Israel. The hesed (H2617) of YHWH is the theological center of the entire book.

verses.flow: And now, YHWH, take, I pray, my life from me, for better is my death than my life."

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And YHWH said, "Is it good that it burns hot to you?"
verses.note: HEBREW: "Is it good that it burns hot to you?" — the verb charah (H2734) means to burn with anger or heat. YHWH's question is left unanswered here, a rhetorical suspension that frames the enacted parable of vv. 5-11. The same question recurs in v. 9, forming a literary bracket.

verses.flow: And YHWH God appointed a plant (qiqayon) and caused it to come up over Jonah, to be a shade over his head, to deliver him from his evil. And Jonah rejoiced over the plant with great joy.
verses.note: HEBREW: The qiqayon (H7021) is a plant of uncertain identification — possibly the castor oil plant (Ricinus communis), a fast-growing leafy shrub native to the ANE. The root "appointed" (manah, H4487) is the same verb used for the great fish (1:17), the worm (4:7), and the east wind (4:8), emphasizing YHWH's sovereign orchestration of all created agents. TYPOLOGY: The plant's sudden appearance and disappearance mirrors the transience of earthly comfort, pointing to the only lasting shelter found in YHWH himself.

verses.flow: But God appointed a worm at the coming up of the dawn on the next day, and it struck the plant so that it withered.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The fourfold use of "appointed" (manah) across chapters 1-4 — fish, plant, worm, wind — frames YHWH as the sovereign Lord of the divine council who deploys all natural and spiritual agents as his servants, subordinate to his redemptive purposes.

verses.flow: And God said to Jonah, "Is it good that it burns hot to you concerning the plant?" And he said, "It is good that it burns hot to me — unto death."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jonah's blunt reply — "It is good that it burns hot to me unto death" — is a confession of idolatrous self-pity. He would rather die than witness Gentile salvation. Jesus uses this same Ninevite repentance as a judgment against unbelieving Israel (Matt 12:41; Luke 11:32), inverting Jonah's contempt into a standard of faith.

verses.flow: And YHWH said, "You yourself had compassion on the plant, for which you did not labor and which you did not cause to grow, which came into being in a night and perished in a night.

words.g: the + in/with a city in the widest sense

verses.flow: And I — shall I not have compassion on Nineveh, the great city, in which there are more than one hundred and twenty thousand persons who do not know the difference between their right hand and their left, and also much cattle?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The closing rhetorical question is unanswered, left open to implicate every reader. The "one hundred and twenty thousand who do not know their right hand from their left" likely refers to children too young for moral discernment, signaling the massive scale of Nineveh's population. YHWH's concern for "much cattle" further grounds divine compassion in creational care, not merely covenantal categories. TYPOLOGY: Christ, the greater Jonah, does not flee from Gentile sinners but pursues them — fulfilling what Jonah only foreshadowed in his reluctant, resentful mission (Matt 12:41; John 3:16; Rom 15:9-12).
